title: Podman always push images to the remote repository
body:
### Issue Description

Hello,

We currently use `docker` to build our images during the CI. The CI use Jenkins and run inside Kubernetes. Since Kubernetes remove the usage of docker we have to change the way we are building our images, since we cannot rely anymore on the node's docker socket.
We decide to replace `docker` using `podman` since it is a drop in replacement.

We notice that the `push` step was a lot longer with `podman` than it was with `docker`.
After looking at the log we see that `podman` always push the image even if it already exist.

Since we are running inside kubernetes each time a pipeline is run it is a new pod that take care of building so there is no cache.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Start podman in container (e.g. docker run -it --rm --device /dev/fuse:/dev/fuse --security-opt seccomp=unconfined --security-opt apparmor=unconfined quay.io/stable/podman)
2. Create a dockerfile
```
FROM alpine:latest
RUN echo "hello"
```
3. podman build --isolation=chroot -t registry.example.com/test_podman .
4. podman push registry.example.com/test_podman
5. Exit the container
6. Start a new container
7.  Create a dockerfile
```
FROM alpine:latest
RUN echo "hello"
```
8.  podman build --isolation=chroot -t registry.example.com/test_podman .
9. podman push registry.example.com/test_podman


### Describe the results you received

The second push also push data instead of detecting that it already exists on the remote repository

In the debug log level we can see that

### Describe the results you expected

The second push should detect that the layers already exists on the remote repository.

### Podman in a container

Yes

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Running in Kubernetes in Jenkins

### Additional information

If I tried to push two times in the same container the second push detect correctly that the layers already exists.

comments.body: It seems that if podman recheck the sha256 against the repository after compressing on the fly should correct this behaviour

Yes; completely new pushes in a new environment, with `blob-info-cache-v1.boltdb` not preserved, are going to compress and push afresh.

I would _guess_ that this is not an issue with Docker primarily because Docker is not actually confined that way, and instead using a remote socket to something with a shared state.

So the primary recommendation would be to use some kind of long-term volume to keep the directory with `blob-info-cache-v1.boltdb` over several builds.

---

Alternatively, the Buildah “blob cache” (something different from the “blob info cache”) could be used to record, and upload, the original compressed versions to the destination, all within the scope of a single in-container build; that would probably save a chunk of time because compression tends to be the slowest part, but it’s not as good as preserving the ”blob info cache”, where an upload is a just a metadata operation.

---

There is _some_ work in progress to avoid uploads in more situations, https://github.com/containers/image/pull/1645 . That is one way in which Docker is currently avoiding uploads, but it’s not _nearly_ as trivial as it may seem in naive experiments — if you _rely_ on the optimization triggering, this logic can break at any time the generator of the of the base image, and the implementation of the push of the derived image, drift away in implementation. So reusing the blob info cache still remains the more useful recommendation.

---

> It seems that if podman recheck the sha256 against the repository after compressing on the fly should correct this behaviour

Typically, compression is _by far_ the slowest part of a push. So compressing, and _then_ checking whether the blob exists, would _always_ slow down uploads where there is no match, and speed up uploads with a match _very little_. That option (`DockerRegistryPushPrecomputeDigests`) exists in the underlying implementation, but it’s almost always a bad tradeoff, so it is not exposed in Podman.

Thank you for your details answer.

So, for now the solution I have is to keep the `blob-info-cache-v1.boltdb` over the builds. Is this DB can be shared between several builds at the same time without a chance to be corrupted or it is not thread safe ?

For my information what's the difference between the "blob info cache" and the Buildah "blob cache" ?

Another thing that I'm not understanding is that it seems that we can compress the image during build with `--disable-compression=false`, when using this option we still have the need to compress blob on the fly when doing the push, why ? Does it have a link with the Buildah "blob cache" or is it another compression for another thing ?

The database is using BoltDB; that relies on `flock` locking. Assuming that works correctly across containers (which might not be the case on a distributed filesystem?), concurrent use should not be an issue.

The difference is that one caches information and another caches whole blobs :)

----

I’m not immediately sure about `build --disable-compression`. My _quick guess_ is that this is inherited from non-`Containerfile` build methods, where the build output can be written ~directly to some external image rather than to the local container storage (e.g. `buildah commit docker://…`), but I haven’t now checked in detail. Either way, as the option description says, the default is appropriate for `podman build`.
Okay thank you for the information, I will take a look at sharing the boltdb caches
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think this one is all set, closing.  Reopen if I am mistaken.
It works by keeping the boltdb caches between builds
